question: An 82-year-old patient presents with progressive recent memory loss and hippocampal atrophy on MRI. Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Alzheimer disease due to cortical neurodegeneration
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes that impaired recent memory from hippocampal and medial temporal atrophy is the most common initial symptom of Alzheimer disease; the document states: "Most common initial symptom of AD is impaired memory of recent events". This pattern matches AD pathophysiology.

options.text: Limbic TDP-43 encephalopathy causing isolated amnesia
options.rationale: Wrong because LATE typically affects patients >80 with limbic TDP-43 pathology and can mimic AD, but the lecture specifies LATE often requires amyloid-negative biomarkers to be probable; hippocampal atrophy alone without TDP-43 confirmation does not establish LATE.

options.text: Vascular cognitive impairment from strategic infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because vascular dementia often shows stepwise decline and infarcts on imaging; the lecture emphasizes multiple infarcts or chronic ischemia rather than isolated hippocampal atrophy for vascular dementia.

options.text: Dementia with Lewy bodies presenting with hallucinations
options.rationale: Wrong because Lewy body dementia typically presents with early attention and visuospatial deficits and visual hallucinations; the lecture notes memory impairment is not the earliest dominant feature.

options.text: Frontotemporal lobar degeneration with behavioral change
options.rationale: Wrong because FTLD commonly causes personality and language changes with frontal/temporal atrophy; isolated hippocampal-predominant memory loss favors AD per the lecture.

question: A 68-year-old with progressive cognitive decline has both neuritic extracellular deposits and intracellular filamentous inclusions on autopsy. Which combination best explains the pathology?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Extracellular amyloid plaques and intracellular tau tangles
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states that neuritic plaques are aggregated Aβ peptides and tangles are aggregates of tau; both must be seen for pathological AD diagnosis.

options.text: Intracellular alpha-synuclein inclusions and amyloid plaques
options.rationale: Wrong because alpha-synuclein Lewy bodies are characteristic of Lewy body disease, not the defining intracellular filamentous tangles of AD; presence of plaques alone would not explain tau tangles.

options.text: TDP-43 cytoplasmic inclusions with vascular amyloid deposition
options.rationale: Wrong because TDP-43 inclusions define LATE or FTLD-TDP, and vascular amyloid (CAA) affects vessels; this combination does not represent the neuritic plaques plus neurofibrillary tangles required for AD.

options.text: Prion protein aggregates with granulovacuolar degeneration
options.rationale: Wrong because prion aggregates cause spongiform change in prion disease; granulovacuolar degeneration can be seen in AD but prion pathology is distinct and would not explain classic AD tangles.

options.text: Hirano bodies and microinfarcts causing cortical loss
options.rationale: Wrong because Hirano bodies are common hippocampal findings but are not the primary neuritic plaques and neurofibrillary tangles that define AD pathology.

question: Which genetic factor from the lecture increases late-onset risk in a dose-dependent manner?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Apolipoprotein E epsilon four allele status
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explicitly identifies apolipoprotein E ε4 as the only late-onset risk factor definitely associated with AD and notes dose-dependent risk.

options.text: Presenilin one mutation causing late-onset disease
options.rationale: Wrong because presenilin mutations are associated with familial early-onset AD (<60 years), not the common late-onset risk described in the lecture.

options.text: Tau gene mutation causing late-onset Alzheimer disease
options.rationale: Wrong because tau mutations cause frontotemporal lobar degeneration rather than typical late-onset AD per the lecture.

options.text: PRNP gene polymorphism increasing Alzheimer risk
options.rationale: Wrong because PRNP mutations relate to prion diseases, not the late-onset AD risk factor emphasized in the lecture.

options.text: APP gene duplication in sporadic late-onset cases
options.rationale: Wrong because APP mutations are linked to familial early-onset AD; sporadic late-onset disease is not explained by APP duplication per the lecture.

question: Which imaging modality from the lecture best documents cortical amyloid burden to guide anti-amyloid therapy decisions?
cited_learning_objective: Alzheimer’s disease blood tests

options.text: Amyloid PET scan showing cortical binding pattern
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states amyloid PET assesses amyloid-β plaques in cortex and is used in patients considered for amyloid-targeted therapy.

options.text: FDG-PET demonstrating parietal hypometabolism pattern
options.rationale: Wrong because FDG-PET reflects hypometabolism and neuronal loss but does not directly measure amyloid plaque burden used to select anti-amyloid therapy.

options.text: T1-weighted MRI showing hippocampal atrophy only
options.rationale: Wrong because MRI documents structural atrophy but does not quantify amyloid plaques required for anti-amyloid therapy decisions.

options.text: Tau PET showing neurofibrillary tangle burden
options.rationale: Wrong because tau PET assesses tangles and correlates with progression but the lecture specifies amyloid PET for documenting amyloid pathology for therapy selection.

options.text: Susceptibility-weighted MRI showing microhemorrhages
options.rationale: Wrong because SWI detects microbleeds and CAA risk but does not measure cortical amyloid plaque burden for anti-amyloid therapy.

question: A primary care clinician considers blood biomarker testing for a patient with mild cognitive deficits. Which plasma marker from the lecture most directly predicts brain amyloid plaque burden?
cited_learning_objective: Alzheimer’s disease blood tests

options.text: Plasma amyloid beta forty two to forty ratio
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists plasma Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio as predicting amyloid plaque burden in the brain.

options.text: Plasma phosphorylated tau two seventeen concentration
options.rationale: Wrong because p-tau217 is strongly associated with amyloid pathology and PET positivity but the lecture identifies Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio as the direct predictor of plaque burden.

options.text: Neurofilament light chain indicating neuronal injury
options.rationale: Wrong because neurofilament light is a marker of neuronal injury and differentiates neurodegenerative from non-neurodegenerative conditions, not a direct predictor of amyloid plaque burden.

options.text: Plasma total tau concentration reflecting plaque load
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture emphasizes phosphorylated tau isoforms and Aβ ratios; total tau is not presented as the primary predictor of plaque burden.

options.text: Cerebrospinal fluid oligoclonal bands indicating inflammation
options.rationale: Wrong because CSF oligoclonal bands indicate inflammatory processes and are not described in the lecture as predictors of amyloid plaque burden.

question: Which neuropathologic vascular finding described in the lecture increases risk for lobar intracerebral hemorrhage?
cited_learning_objective: Review cerebral amyloid angiopathy and vascular dementia (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Amyloid beta deposition in cortical vessel walls
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explains that deposition of Aβ peptide in vessel walls (CAA) makes them fragile and increases risk for lobar hemorrhages.

options.text: Hyaline arteriolosclerosis of deep penetrating arteries
options.rationale: Wrong because hyaline arteriolosclerosis is linked to small vessel ischemic disease and lacunar infarcts rather than lobar hemorrhages from cortical amyloid deposition.

options.text: Large artery atherosclerotic plaque causing embolic stroke
options.rationale: Wrong because large artery atherosclerosis causes ischemic strokes and infarcts, not the lobar hemorrhages associated with amyloid-laden cortical vessels.

options.text: Venous sinus thrombosis producing cortical hemorrhagic infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because venous sinus thrombosis leads to venous infarcts and hemorrhage but is not the amyloid-related vessel fragility described in the lecture.

options.text: Capillary telangiectasia causing focal microbleeds
options.rationale: Wrong because capillary telangiectasia is a vascular malformation distinct from amyloid deposition and is not the common cause of lobar hemorrhages discussed in the lecture.

question: Which clinical feature from the lecture helps distinguish dementia with Lewy bodies from Alzheimer disease early in the course?
cited_learning_objective: Review Lewy body disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Early visuospatial deficits with visual hallucinations
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes that impairment of attention, executive and visuospatial function and visual hallucinations (>75%) occur before memory loss in dementia with Lewy bodies.

options.text: Prominent early episodic memory loss without hallucinations
options.rationale: Wrong because early episodic memory loss without hallucinations is more characteristic of Alzheimer disease per the lecture.

options.text: Stepwise cognitive decline after multiple infarcts
options.rationale: Wrong because stepwise decline after infarcts describes vascular dementia, not Lewy body disease.

options.text: Early expressive aphasia with semantic deficits
options.rationale: Wrong because early language-predominant syndromes suggest frontotemporal lobar degeneration rather than Lewy body disease.

options.text: Rapid progression with myoclonus and spongiform change
options.rationale: Wrong because rapid progression with myoclonus and spongiform pathology indicates prion disease, not Lewy body dementia.

question: A neuropathology report shows loss of normal nuclear immunoreactivity and cytoplasmic phosphorylated inclusions in limbic neurons of an 85-year-old. Which staging progression from the lecture best matches this TDP-43 distribution?
cited_learning_objective: Limbic-predominant age-related TDP-43 encephalopathy (LATE), also referred to as TDP-43 encephalopathy

options.text: Stage one amygdala then hippocampus then frontal cortex
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes LATE staging as Stage 1: amygdala only; Stage 2: + hippocampus; Stage 3: + middle frontal gyrus.

options.text: Stage one hippocampus then amygdala then occipital cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture specifies initial involvement of the amygdala followed by hippocampus, not hippocampus first or occipital involvement.

options.text: Diffuse cortical involvement without limbic predominance
options.rationale: Wrong because LATE is limbic-predominant and the lecture emphasizes limbic distribution rather than diffuse cortical-only disease.

options.text: Subcortical basal ganglia predominant TDP-43 pathology
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture indicates limbic structures are primarily affected in LATE, not basal ganglia predominance.

options.text: Vascular perivascular TDP-43 deposition causing infarcts
options.rationale: Wrong because TDP-43 proteinopathy is neuronal and glial, not described as perivascular deposition causing infarcts in the lecture.

question: Which neuropathologic finding listed in the lecture is commonly seen in hippocampal pyramidal neurons and composed of actin?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Hirano bodies appearing as paracrystalline actin inclusions
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states Hirano bodies are most commonly seen in hippocampal pyramidal neurons, composed of actin and appear paracrystalline.

options.text: Granulovacuolar degeneration with tau-positive inclusions
options.rationale: Wrong because granulovacuolar degeneration consists of cytoplasmic vacuoles with granules and is not described as actin-based Hirano bodies in the lecture.

options.text: Pick bodies composed of alpha-synuclein aggregates
options.rationale: Wrong because Pick bodies are tau-positive inclusions in FTLD-tau, not actin-based Hirano bodies; alpha-synuclein composes Lewy bodies, not Pick bodies.

options.text: Lewy bodies in hippocampus composed of tau protein
options.rationale: Wrong because Lewy bodies are primarily alpha-synuclein aggregates, not tau or actin-based Hirano bodies as described in the lecture.

options.text: Spongiform vacuoles from prion protein deposition
options.rationale: Wrong because spongiform change in prion disease is vacuolation, distinct from actin-containing Hirano bodies described in the lecture.

question: Which clinical scenario from the lecture would most strongly prompt consideration of prion disease?
cited_learning_objective: Discuss rapidly progressive dementias and prion disorders (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Rapid cognitive decline under two years with myoclonus
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists rapid onset (<2 years), motor symptoms, ataxia, visual abnormalities, and myoclonus as concerning for prion disease such as CJD.

options.text: Gradual memory loss over a decade with hippocampal atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong because gradual long-term decline with hippocampal atrophy is more typical of Alzheimer disease rather than rapidly progressive prion disorders per the lecture.

options.text: Stepwise decline after multiple small cortical infarcts
options.rationale: Wrong because stepwise decline following infarcts indicates vascular dementia, not prion disease as described in the lecture.

options.text: Early personality change with frontal lobe atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong because early personality and frontal atrophy suggest frontotemporal lobar degeneration, not prion disease.

options.text: Visual hallucinations with fluctuating attention and parkinsonism
options.rationale: Wrong because visual hallucinations with fluctuating cognition and parkinsonism point toward Lewy body dementia, not prion disease.

question: A 74-year-old with progressive memory loss has an amyloid PET that is negative. According to the lecture, which diagnosis becomes more likely if hippocampal neurodegeneration is disproportionate?
cited_learning_objective: Limbic-predominant age-related TDP-43 encephalopathy (LATE), also referred to as TDP-43 encephalopathy

options.text: Possible limbic-predominant TDP-43 encephalopathy
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture defines probable LATE as amyloid biomarker-negative and possible LATE when amyloid biomarkers are unavailable or amyloid-positive but hippocampal degeneration is disproportionate; a negative amyloid PET with disproportionate hippocampal atrophy supports LATE.

options.text: Alzheimer disease despite negative amyloid imaging
options.rationale: Wrong because AD typically shows amyloid pathology; a negative amyloid PET reduces the likelihood of AD per the lecture.

options.text: Vascular dementia from chronic small vessel disease
options.rationale: Wrong because vascular dementia is suggested by infarcts or white matter ischemic change rather than isolated disproportionate hippocampal neurodegeneration described here.

options.text: Frontotemporal lobar degeneration with tauopathy
options.rationale: Wrong because FTLD-tau presents with frontal/temporal cortical atrophy and behavioral or language syndromes, not isolated hippocampal-predominant atrophy with negative amyloid PET.

options.text: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease causing rapid hippocampal loss
options.rationale: Wrong because prion diseases present with rapid progression and spongiform change; the lecture indicates LATE is the limbic TDP-43 disorder matching this amyloid-negative hippocampal pattern.

question: Which PET pattern described in the lecture is characteristic of dementia with Lewy bodies?
cited_learning_objective: Review Lewy body disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Parieto-occipital hypometabolism with cingulate island sign
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes bilateral parieto-occipital hypometabolism with relative preservation of posterior cingulate metabolism (cingulate island sign) in dementia with Lewy bodies.

options.text: Diffuse frontal hypometabolism with frontal atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong because diffuse frontal hypometabolism is more typical of behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia, not the parieto-occipital pattern of Lewy body disease.

options.text: Medial temporal hypometabolism isolated to hippocampus
options.rationale: Wrong because medial temporal hypometabolism is characteristic of Alzheimer disease rather than the parieto-occipital pattern described for Lewy body dementia.

options.text: Global cortical hypometabolism without regional preservation
options.rationale: Wrong because global hypometabolism lacks the regional signature (cingulate island) that helps distinguish Lewy body dementia per the lecture.

options.text: Occipital hypermetabolism with preserved parietal cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture describes occipital-parietal hypometabolism in Lewy body disease, not occipital hypermetabolism.

question: Which statement from the lecture best reflects the definitive method to diagnose Alzheimer disease?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Autopsy examination demonstrating plaques and tangles
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states the only definite way to diagnose AD and other neurodegenerative diseases is through autopsy examination showing neuritic plaques and neurofibrillary tangles.

options.text: Clinical cognitive testing alone without imaging
options.rationale: Wrong because dementia is a clinical diagnosis but the lecture emphasizes that definitive diagnosis requires neuropathologic confirmation at autopsy.

options.text: Negative amyloid PET excluding Alzheimer disease
options.rationale: Wrong because while a negative amyloid PET has high negative predictive value, the lecture specifies autopsy as the only definitive diagnostic method.

options.text: Plasma p-tau217 elevation confirming Alzheimer disease
options.rationale: Wrong because p-tau217 correlates with amyloid pathology and PET positivity but is not the definitive autopsy diagnosis described in the lecture.

options.text: MRI hippocampal atrophy proving Alzheimer disease
options.rationale: Wrong because MRI shows structural changes but cannot definitively diagnose AD without neuropathologic confirmation per the lecture.

question: Which vascular pathology mechanism from the lecture explains white matter degeneration leading to cognitive impairment?
cited_learning_objective: Review cerebral amyloid angiopathy and vascular dementia (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Small vessel disease causing hypoxic white matter injury
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes small vessel disease leading to hypoxic environments, oligodendrocyte and myelin loss, and white matter degeneration contributing to cognitive impairment.

options.text: Large cortical emboli causing isolated hippocampal loss
options.rationale: Wrong because large embolic infarcts cause focal cortical infarction rather than the diffuse white matter changes from small vessel disease emphasized in the lecture.

options.text: Venous congestion producing periventricular edema only
options.rationale: Wrong because venous congestion and edema are not the primary small vessel hypoxic mechanisms described as causing white matter degeneration in the lecture.

options.text: Capillary proliferation improving white matter perfusion
options.rationale: Wrong because capillary proliferation would not explain the demyelination and lacunar infarcts associated with small vessel disease described in the lecture.

options.text: Arteriovenous malformation causing chronic ischemia
options.rationale: Wrong because AVMs are focal vascular malformations and are not the progressive small vessel changes leading to diffuse white matter degeneration discussed in the lecture.

question: Which clinical feature from the lecture most helps differentiate frontotemporal lobar degeneration from Alzheimer disease early on?
cited_learning_objective: Briefly highlight the neuropathological aspects of a select set of frontotemporal degenerations (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Early personality and behavioral changes with frontal atrophy
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists behavioral variant FTD with personality change and frontal lobe atrophy as distinguishing features from AD, which typically begins with memory impairment.

options.text: Early episodic memory loss with hippocampal atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong because early episodic memory loss and hippocampal atrophy are more characteristic of Alzheimer disease per the lecture.

options.text: Visual hallucinations preceding cognitive decline
options.rationale: Wrong because visual hallucinations are more typical of dementia with Lewy bodies, not frontotemporal lobar degeneration.

options.text: Rapid myoclonus and spongiform cortical change
options.rationale: Wrong because rapid myoclonus and spongiform change indicate prion disease, not FTLD.

options.text: Stepwise decline after multiple lacunar infarcts
options.rationale: Wrong because stepwise decline after infarcts suggests vascular dementia rather than FTLD as described in the lecture.

question: Which protein aggregate is the primary component of Lewy bodies according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Review Lewy body disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Alpha-synuclein comprising Lewy body inclusions
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states Lewy bodies are primarily composed of alpha-synuclein.

options.text: Hyperphosphorylated tau forming Lewy bodies
options.rationale: Wrong because tau aggregates form neurofibrillary tangles in AD and FTLD-tau, not Lewy bodies which are alpha-synuclein based per the lecture.

options.text: TDP-43 inclusions forming Lewy body pathology
options.rationale: Wrong because TDP-43 inclusions define LATE and FTLD-TDP, not Lewy body disease as described in the lecture.

options.text: PrPsc prion protein composing Lewy bodies
options.rationale: Wrong because prion protein aggregates cause prion diseases and spongiform change, not Lewy bodies which are alpha-synuclein based.

options.text: Amyloid beta fibrils forming Lewy body cores
options.rationale: Wrong because amyloid beta forms extracellular plaques and vascular amyloid, not the intracellular Lewy body inclusions composed of alpha-synuclein.

question: Which diagnostic approach from the lecture is recommended for primary care clinicians to identify high-risk patients earlier using scalable testing?
cited_learning_objective: Alzheimer’s disease blood tests

options.text: Blood biomarker testing for plasma amyloid and p-tau
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture highlights blood biomarker testing (plasma Aβ42/Aβ40 and p-tau) as noninvasive, scalable tools primary care can use to identify high-risk patients earlier.

options.text: Routine CSF analysis for all patients with mild complaints
options.rationale: Wrong because CSF analysis is more invasive; the lecture emphasizes blood biomarkers as more scalable and accessible for primary care.

options.text: Immediate amyloid PET for every cognitive complaint
options.rationale: Wrong because amyloid PET is costly and less scalable; the lecture suggests blood biomarkers as a first-line scalable approach.

options.text: Genetic testing for presenilin mutations in all elders
options.rationale: Wrong because presenilin mutations are rare familial early-onset causes; the lecture does not recommend universal genetic testing in primary care.

options.text: Electroencephalography for screening neurodegeneration
options.rationale: Wrong because EEG is not presented in the lecture as a scalable primary care screening tool for AD; blood biomarkers are recommended instead.

question: A 79-year-old with cognitive decline has numerous cortical microbleeds on susceptibility-weighted MRI. Which pathology from the lecture most likely explains these findings?
cited_learning_objective: Review cerebral amyloid angiopathy and vascular dementia (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy with amyloid in vessel walls
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explains that deposition of Aβ peptide in vessel walls (CAA) leads to fragile vessels and cortical microhemorrhages visible as blooming artifacts on SWI.

options.text: Hypertensive arteriolosclerosis causing deep microbleeds
options.rationale: Wrong because hypertensive arteriolosclerosis typically causes deep subcortical microbleeds, whereas cortical microbleeds are characteristic of CAA per the lecture.

options.text: Cerebral amyloid plaques causing parenchymal calcifications
options.rationale: Wrong because amyloid plaques are extracellular parenchymal deposits and do not directly explain cortical microbleeds from vessel wall amyloid described in CAA.

options.text: Venous angiopathy producing superficial siderosis only
options.rationale: Wrong because venous angiopathy and superficial siderosis are distinct entities and the lecture links cortical microbleeds specifically to amyloid-laden vessel fragility.

options.text: Microembolic shower from cardiac source causing cortical lesions
options.rationale: Wrong because microemboli produce ischemic infarcts rather than the diffuse cortical microhemorrhages associated with amyloid angiopathy described in the lecture.

question: Which statement from the lecture best summarizes the relationship between plaques and tangles in Alzheimer disease?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Altered APP metabolism triggers amyloid then tau pathology
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture indicates alterations in APP metabolism increasing Aβ secretion or decreased clearance trigger mechanisms leading to tau pathology and neuronal death.

options.text: Tau mutations always precede amyloid deposition in AD
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture notes mutations of tau cause FTLD without amyloid accumulation; tau mutations do not universally precede amyloid in AD.

options.text: Plaques alone determine severity of cognitive decline
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture states neurofibrillary tangle number correlates better with dementia severity than neuritic plaque number.

options.text: Amyloid deposition is unrelated to tau aggregation
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture suggests amyloid alterations can trigger downstream tau pathology, indicating a relationship between the two.

options.text: Tangles are extracellular deposits of amyloid peptides
options.rationale: Wrong because tangles are intracellular aggregates of tau, not extracellular amyloid peptide deposits which form plaques as described in the lecture.

question: Which diagnostic imaging finding from the lecture is most consistent with normal pressure hydrocephalus presenting with dementia and gait disturbance?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Ventricular enlargement with gait ataxia and urinary symptoms
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture references a case of normal pressure hydrocephalus with gait ataxia, urinary incontinence, and dementia and notes ventriculomegaly as an imaging feature.

options.text: Temporal horn dilation with isolated memory impairment
options.rationale: Wrong because temporal horn dilation and hippocampal atrophy are more characteristic of Alzheimer disease rather than the triad of NPH described in the lecture.

options.text: Diffuse cortical atrophy with widened sulci only
options.rationale: Wrong because diffuse cortical atrophy is a nonspecific finding seen in many dementias and does not specifically indicate NPH with gait disturbance.

options.text: Focal mass with vasogenic edema causing midline shift
options.rationale: Wrong because a mass lesion with vasogenic edema and midline shift suggests tumor or hemorrhage, not NPH as discussed in the lecture.

options.text: Multiple lacunar infarcts in basal ganglia causing stepwise decline
options.rationale: Wrong because lacunar infarcts and stepwise decline indicate vascular dementia, not the ventricular enlargement and gait triad of NPH.

question: Which autopsy technique from the lecture is emphasized as ideal when evaluating suspected prion disease?
cited_learning_objective: Discuss rapidly progressive dementias and prion disorders (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Obtain both formalin-fixed and fresh brain tissue samples
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states that ideally both formalin-fixed and fresh tissue are available for prion disease testing including immunohistochemistry and Western blot.

options.text: Only formalin-fixed tissue is sufficient for all tests
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture emphasizes fresh tissue is needed for certain assays like Western blot and definitive prion testing.

options.text: Peripheral blood PCR replaces brain tissue examination
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture specifies brain tissue examination and specialized testing at the National Prion Disease Pathology Surveillance Center, not peripheral blood PCR as a substitute.

options.text: Skin biopsy immunostaining is diagnostic for prion disease
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture focuses on brain tissue immunohistochemistry and Western blot for prion protein, not skin biopsy as the diagnostic standard.

options.text: CSF cytology alone confirms prion pathology
options.rationale: Wrong because CSF studies may help but the lecture indicates brain tissue and specialized testing are required for definitive prion diagnosis.

question: Which epidemiologic fact from the lecture about Alzheimer disease is correct?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Alzheimer disease is the most common cause of dementia in elderly
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture opens by stating AD is the most common cause of dementia in the elderly.

options.text: Alzheimer disease incidence decreases after age eighty four
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture reports incidence increases with age, with >84 years showing approximately 47% incidence.

options.text: Alzheimer disease is primarily a disease of young adults
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture notes that in >90% of people with AD, symptoms do not appear until after age 60.

options.text: Alzheimer disease has negligible economic impact on healthcare
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture cites substantial costs (≈$360 billion in 2024) and unpaid caregiving value, indicating major economic impact.

options.text: Alzheimer disease is always familial and autosomal dominant
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture explains inheritance is genetically complex with most cases sporadic; familial autosomal dominant early-onset is a minority.

question: Which neuropathologic feature from the lecture correlates best with degree of dementia severity?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Neurofibrillary tangle burden correlates with dementia severity
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states neurofibrillary tangle number correlates better with degree of dementia than neuritic plaque number.

options.text: Neuritic plaque count alone determines cognitive impairment
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture indicates plaques contribute but tangles correlate more closely with dementia severity.

options.text: Hirano body quantity directly predicts global cognition
options.rationale: Wrong because Hirano bodies are common but are not presented as correlating directly with dementia severity in the lecture.

options.text: Microinfarct number is irrelevant to cognitive decline
options.rationale: Wrong because microinfarcts and vascular pathology can contribute to cognitive impairment per the lecture; they are not irrelevant.

options.text: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy severity equals tangle burden
options.rationale: Wrong because CAA affects vessels and hemorrhage risk; the lecture does not equate CAA severity with neurofibrillary tangle burden.

question: A 76-year-old with progressive memory loss has plasma p-tau217 elevated and positive amyloid PET. According to the lecture, which interpretation is most consistent?
cited_learning_objective: Alzheimer’s disease blood tests

options.text: Biomarkers indicate Alzheimer pathology and higher PET positivity
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes p-tau217 has the highest accuracy in predicting amyloid PET positivity and is strongly associated with amyloid pathology, supporting AD diagnosis.

options.text: Elevated p-tau217 excludes Alzheimer disease
options.rationale: Wrong because elevated p-tau217 is associated with amyloid pathology and PET positivity, not exclusion of AD as per the lecture.

options.text: Positive amyloid PET indicates pure vascular dementia
options.rationale: Wrong because amyloid PET documents amyloid-β plaques characteristic of AD, not vascular dementia which is due to infarcts and ischemia per the lecture.

options.text: Neurofilament light elevation alone confirms nondegenerative cause
options.rationale: Wrong because neurofilament light is a marker of neuronal injury and differentiates neurodegenerative from non-neurodegenerative conditions, but alone does not confirm a nondegenerative cause.

options.text: Negative tau PET would prove absence of Alzheimer disease
options.rationale: Wrong because tau PET correlates with tangles and progression but the lecture does not state a negative tau PET definitively rules out AD when amyloid and p-tau217 are positive.

question: Which clinical vignette from the lecture best suggests vascular dementia rather than Alzheimer disease?
cited_learning_objective: Review cerebral amyloid angiopathy and vascular dementia (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Stepwise cognitive decline after multiple small infarcts
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes vascular dementia as resulting from multiple infarcts or chronic ischemia with a stepwise decline in cognition.

options.text: Gradual isolated episodic memory loss with hippocampal atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong because gradual isolated memory loss with hippocampal atrophy is more typical of Alzheimer disease per the lecture.

options.text: Early visual hallucinations with fluctuating attention
options.rationale: Wrong because early visual hallucinations and fluctuating cognition suggest dementia with Lewy bodies, not vascular dementia.

options.text: Rapid myoclonus and cortical ribbon sign on DWI
options.rationale: Wrong because rapid myoclonus and cortical ribbon DWI changes indicate prion disease such as CJD, not vascular dementia.

options.text: Personality change with frontal lobe hypometabolism
options.rationale: Wrong because personality change and frontal hypometabolism point toward frontotemporal lobar degeneration rather than vascular dementia.

question: Which neuropathologic stain from the lecture is used to demonstrate amyloid in vessel walls and shows apple-green birefringence under polarized light?
cited_learning_objective: Review cerebral amyloid angiopathy and vascular dementia (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Congo red stain viewed with polarization microscopy
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states amyloid in vessel walls stains orange or red with Congo red and is birefringent under polarized light, a classic method to identify amyloid.

options.text: Luxol fast blue stain for myelin loss
options.rationale: Wrong because Luxol fast blue stains myelin and is used for white matter changes, not for demonstrating amyloid birefringence in vessel walls.

options.text: Silver stain highlighting neurofibrillary tangles
options.rationale: Wrong because silver stains can highlight tangles or Pick bodies but Congo red is the specific stain for amyloid with polarization properties as described in the lecture.

options.text: Hematoxylin and eosin showing spongiform change
options.rationale: Wrong because H&E can show general histology and spongiform change in prion disease but does not demonstrate amyloid birefringence under polarized light.

options.text: Anti-synuclein immunostain for Lewy bodies
options.rationale: Wrong because anti-synuclein IHC detects Lewy bodies, not vascular amyloid demonstrated by Congo red polarization per the lecture.

question: Which clinical feature from the lecture is most characteristic of dementia with Lewy bodies and helps distinguish it from Parkinson disease dementia based on timing?
cited_learning_objective: Review Lewy body disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Cognitive symptoms onset within one year of motor signs
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture defines dementia with Lewy bodies when cognitive and motor symptom onset are less than one year apart, distinguishing it from Parkinson disease dementia.

options.text: Motor symptoms preceding cognition by more than five years
options.rationale: Wrong because long-standing motor symptoms preceding cognitive decline suggest Parkinson disease with later dementia, not dementia with Lewy bodies per the lecture.

options.text: Early isolated memory loss without motor signs
options.rationale: Wrong because early isolated memory loss without motor signs is more typical of Alzheimer disease rather than the timing-based distinction for Lewy body dementia.

options.text: Rapid progression with myoclonus and ataxia
options.rationale: Wrong because rapid progression with myoclonus and ataxia suggests prion disease, not the Lewy body timing distinction discussed in the lecture.

options.text: Stepwise decline after multiple cortical infarcts
options.rationale: Wrong because stepwise decline after infarcts indicates vascular dementia, not the timing-based Lewy body versus Parkinson disease dementia distinction.

question: Which neuropathologic entity from the lecture is most often associated with early-onset familial Alzheimer disease under age 60?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Mutations in presenilin one or presenilin two genes
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists presenilin 1 and presenilin 2 mutations (and APP mutations) as causes of familial early-onset AD (<60 years).

options.text: Apolipoprotein E epsilon four homozygosity only
options.rationale: Wrong because APOE ε4 increases late-onset risk dose-dependently but familial early-onset AD is linked to presenilin and APP mutations per the lecture.

options.text: Tau gene mutation causing early Alzheimer disease
options.rationale: Wrong because tau mutations cause FTLD rather than familial early-onset AD according to the lecture.

options.text: PRNP mutation causing familial Alzheimer phenotype
options.rationale: Wrong because PRNP mutations cause prion diseases, not familial early-onset AD as described in the lecture.

options.text: TDP-43 gene mutation producing hippocampal plaques
options.rationale: Wrong because TDP-43 proteinopathy defines LATE or FTLD-TDP, not familial early-onset AD driven by presenilin or APP mutations.

question: Which imaging biomarker from the lecture approximates Braak staging in vivo by assessing neurofibrillary tangle burden?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Tau PET imaging assessing hyperphosphorylated tau burden
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes tau PET as an emerging technique that assesses neurofibrillary tangle burden and approximates Braak staging in the living brain.

options.text: Amyloid PET imaging measuring cortical Aβ plaques
options.rationale: Wrong because amyloid PET measures Aβ plaque burden and is not the modality that approximates Braak staging of tau pathology per the lecture.

options.text: FDG-PET showing regional hypometabolism only
options.rationale: Wrong because FDG-PET reflects hypometabolism and neuronal loss but does not directly approximate Braak staging of tau tangles as tau PET does.

options.text: T1-weighted MRI quantifying ventricular size
options.rationale: Wrong because MRI structural measures like ventricular enlargement do not approximate Braak staging of neurofibrillary tangles described in the lecture.

options.text: SWI MRI detecting microhemorrhages from CAA
options.rationale: Wrong because SWI detects microbleeds related to CAA, not neurofibrillary tangle burden or Braak staging.

question: Which neuropathologic description from the lecture best characterizes granulovacuolar degeneration?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Cytoplasmic vacuole containing a granule in hippocampus
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture defines granulovacuolar degeneration as a cytoplasmic vacuole containing a granule, commonly seen in hippocampus.

options.text: Round silver-staining neuronal inclusions in cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because round silver-staining inclusions (Pick bodies) are characteristic of Pick disease, not granulovacuolar degeneration.

options.text: Paracrystalline actin inclusions called Hirano bodies
options.rationale: Wrong because Hirano bodies are actin-based paracrystalline inclusions distinct from granulovacuolar degeneration as described in the lecture.

options.text: Extracellular amyloid plaques in the neuropil
options.rationale: Wrong because extracellular amyloid plaques are aggregated Aβ peptides, not the intracellular vacuolar granules of granulovacuolar degeneration.

options.text: Spongiform vacuoles from prion protein deposition
options.rationale: Wrong because spongiform change in prion disease is different from granulovacuolar degeneration described in the lecture.

question: Which clinical scenario from the lecture would most strongly suggest LATE rather than pure Alzheimer disease?
cited_learning_objective: Limbic-predominant age-related TDP-43 encephalopathy (LATE), also referred to as TDP-43 encephalopathy

options.text: Elderly patient with predominant amnestic syndrome and negative amyloid biomarkers
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture indicates probable LATE is amyloid biomarker-negative and presents with predominant memory impairment in older adults, distinguishing it from AD.

options.text: Middle-aged patient with early behavioral disinhibition and frontal atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong because early behavioral disinhibition and frontal atrophy suggest frontotemporal lobar degeneration, not LATE which is limbic-predominant in very old adults.

options.text: Patient with fluctuating attention and visual hallucinations
options.rationale: Wrong because fluctuating attention and visual hallucinations point toward dementia with Lewy bodies rather than LATE per the lecture.

options.text: Rapidly progressive dementia with myoclonus and DWI cortical ribbon
options.rationale: Wrong because rapid progression with myoclonus and cortical ribbon on DWI indicates prion disease, not LATE.

options.text: Stepwise decline after multiple lacunar strokes on MRI
options.rationale: Wrong because stepwise decline after lacunar strokes indicates vascular dementia, not the limbic TDP-43 pathology of LATE.

question: Which pathological process from the lecture explains how PrPsc propagates disease in prion disorders?
cited_learning_objective: Discuss rapidly progressive dementias and prion disorders (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Misfolded PrPsc converts normal PrPc into pathogenic form
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes PrPsc converting additional PrPc molecules into PrPsc through physical interaction, leading to pathogenic aggregates.

options.text: Autoimmune antibodies targeting PrPc causing degeneration
options.rationale: Wrong because prion pathogenesis involves protein misfolding and templated conversion, not autoimmune antibody-mediated destruction as per the lecture.

options.text: Viral infection inserting prion genes into neurons
options.rationale: Wrong because prion diseases are caused by protein conformational change and propagation, not viral insertion of prion genes according to the lecture.

options.text: Ischemic necrosis releasing prion precursors extracellularly
options.rationale: Wrong because ischemia is not the mechanism of prion propagation; the lecture emphasizes templated protein misfolding of PrP.

options.text: TDP-43 aggregation seeding prion protein misfolding
options.rationale: Wrong because TDP-43 proteinopathy is distinct from prion protein misfolding; the lecture separates these mechanisms.

question: Which diagnostic feature from the lecture is characteristic on diffusion-weighted MRI for Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease?
cited_learning_objective: Discuss rapidly progressive dementias and prion disorders (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Gyriform cortical hyperintensity and striatal involvement
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes DWI findings in CJD including gyriform cortical hyperintensity (cortical ribbon sign) and symmetric striatal hyperintensity.

options.text: Medial temporal atrophy with temporal horn dilation
options.rationale: Wrong because medial temporal atrophy and temporal horn dilation are structural MRI features more typical of Alzheimer disease, not the DWI cortical ribbon of CJD.

options.text: Parieto-occipital hypometabolism on FDG-PET only
options.rationale: Wrong because FDG-PET hypometabolism patterns are used for neurodegenerative diseases like Lewy body dementia, not the diffusion-weighted cortical ribbon sign of CJD.

options.text: Numerous cortical microbleeds on SWI sequences
options.rationale: Wrong because cortical microbleeds on SWI suggest CAA, not the DWI gyriform hyperintensity characteristic of CJD.

options.text: Diffuse white matter hyperintensity consistent with small vessel disease
options.rationale: Wrong because diffuse white matter changes indicate small vessel ischemic disease, not the acute DWI cortical ribbon and striatal changes seen in CJD.

question: Which clinical population from the lecture is most commonly affected by LATE neuropathological change?
cited_learning_objective: Limbic-predominant age-related TDP-43 encephalopathy (LATE), also referred to as TDP-43 encephalopathy

options.text: Older adults typically over eighty years of age
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states LATE predominantly affects older adults, typically >80 years.

options.text: Young adults in their thirties with familial history
options.rationale: Wrong because LATE is age-related and predominantly affects very old adults, not young adults as per the lecture.

options.text: Middle-aged patients with early-onset dementia
options.rationale: Wrong because early-onset familial dementias are linked to presenilin or APP mutations, not LATE which is age-related in the elderly.

options.text: Children with developmental cognitive disorders
options.rationale: Wrong because LATE is an age-related TDP-43 encephalopathy of older adults, not pediatric developmental disorders.

options.text: Patients with acute infectious encephalitis
options.rationale: Wrong because LATE is a chronic age-related proteinopathy, not an acute infectious encephalitis described in the lecture.

question: Which statement from the lecture best describes the role of neurofilament light chain as a blood biomarker?
cited_learning_objective: Alzheimer’s disease blood tests

options.text: Marker of neuronal injury differentiating conditions
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists neurofilament light chain as a marker of neuronal injury that helps differentiate AD from non-neurodegenerative conditions.

options.text: Direct predictor of amyloid plaque burden in cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture attributes prediction of amyloid plaque burden to plasma Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio, not neurofilament light chain.

options.text: Specific indicator of TDP-43 proteinopathy presence
options.rationale: Wrong because neurofilament light indicates neuronal injury broadly and is not specific for TDP-43 pathology per the lecture.

options.text: Replacement for amyloid PET in all diagnostic contexts
options.rationale: Wrong because the lecture presents blood biomarkers as complementary and scalable but does not state neurofilament light replaces amyloid PET in all contexts.

options.text: Marker exclusively elevated in prion diseases only
options.rationale: Wrong because neurofilament light is a general neuronal injury marker and not exclusive to prion diseases according to the lecture.

question: Which neuropathologic category from the lecture accounts for approximately 45% of frontotemporal lobar degenerations?
cited_learning_objective: Briefly highlight the neuropathological aspects of a select set of frontotemporal degenerations (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: FTLD-tau proteinopathies including Pick disease
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states FTLD-tau comprises about 45% of FTLDs and includes Pick disease, corticobasal degeneration, and progressive supranuclear palsy.

options.text: FTLD-TDP proteinopathies comprising fifty five percent
options.rationale: Wrong because FTLD-TDP accounts for approximately 55% per the lecture, not 45% which is FTLD-tau.

options.text: Prion-related frontotemporal degeneration majority
options.rationale: Wrong because prion diseases are distinct and not the major category of FTLD described in the lecture.

options.text: Alpha-synuclein predominant frontotemporal cases
options.rationale: Wrong because alpha-synuclein Lewy body pathology is separate from FTLD-tau and FTLD-TDP categories discussed in the lecture.

options.text: Vascular small vessel disease causing FTLD pattern
options.rationale: Wrong because vascular small vessel disease causes vascular cognitive impairment, not the FTLD proteinopathy categories outlined in the lecture.

question: Which clinical test domain from the lecture is NOT one of the six DSM-5 cognitive domains used to assess dementia?
cited_learning_objective: Review the basic epidemiology and pathophysiology of Alzheimer’s disease (AACOM competencies I.2.b; I.2.d; II.1.c; Course goal mapping 1, 5)

options.text: Olfactory discrimination is not listed among the six domains
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists learning and memory, language, executive function, complex attention, perceptual motor, and social cognition as the six DSM-5 domains; olfaction is not among them.

options.text: Learning and memory is one of the six DSM-5 domains
options.rationale: Wrong because learning and memory is explicitly listed as one of the six cognitive domains in the lecture.

options.text: Executive function is one of the six DSM-5 domains
options.rationale: Wrong because executive function is explicitly listed among the six domains in the lecture.

options.text: Perceptual motor function is one of the six DSM-5 domains
options.rationale: Wrong because perceptual motor is explicitly included in the six domains per the lecture.

options.text: Social cognition is one of the six DSM-5 domains
options.rationale: Wrong because social cognition is explicitly listed as one of the six DSM-5 cognitive domains in the lecture.
